channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Guerrilla Battle Over Padlocks and Tourist Trash on the Brooklyn Bridge
item.description: Love Locks left behind were one thing. But when actual garbage (like tampons and condoms) started appearing, a righteous quest to remove it was born.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Maag

media:content.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Ellen Baum on a regular visit to the fence on the Brooklyn Bridge that she keeps free of garbage.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Hints for March 14, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: How John Slattery, the ‘Mad Men’ Star, Does Whatever He Wants
item.description: Mr. Slattery makes plans, and then he breaks them. His fans still find him anywhere he goes.
item.dc:creator: Addie Morfoot

media:content.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times

item.title: Saturday Night at the Golden Corral, the Bronx Way
item.description: Two generations of one family have owned this restaurant in the Bronx, trying to change with the times while staying true to the borough.
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia

media:content.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Members of the Harpaul family, who own and operate Golden Corral, including Chandra Harpaul left, and Priya Harpaul, right, dine together at the restaurant on Saturday night.

item.title: Forget ‘Marty Supreme.’ Meet the Real Table Tennis Champion of New York.
item.description: Yasiris Ortiz, a 28-year-old star from the Bronx, is helping to bring free table tennis programing to children across the city.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy and Jonah Markowitz

media:content.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times

item.title: Messi, Trump y un debate político en Argentina
item.description: Lionel Messi, la estrella argentina de fútbol, acompañó a su equipo, el Inter de Miami, a la Casa Blanca. El acontecimiento se convirtió en dinamita política en Argentina.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Daniel Politi

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: El presidente Trump con Lionel Messi y otros jugadores del Inter de Miami en Washington la semana pasada.

item.title: What to Know About Iran’s Kharg Island
item.description: The Persian Gulf island, targeted by U.S. strikes, is home to Iran’s main oil export terminal. Any disruption could jolt global energy markets.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu Agency, via Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Smoke billowing from a flare stack at the Port of Kharg Island oil terminal in Iran in 2017.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. Says It Bombed Key Iranian Island With Oil Hub
item.description: President Trump said he directed the Pentagon to “totally obliterate” military assets on Kharg Island. The U.S. said it was deploying thousands of Marines to the region.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times

item.title: 6 hábitos diurnos para dormir mejor
item.description: Los expertos afirman que un buen descanso nocturno empieza en cuanto te despiertas.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Schupak

media:content.media:credit: Jordan Bohannon; Fotografías de Getty

item.title: His Film Is Spain’s Submission to the Oscars. He’s Not Sure How Spanish It Is.
item.description: Spanish cinema has entered a new and more diverse era, film experts say. Oliver Laxe, the director of Oscar-nominated “Sirat,” embodies the shift.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz

media:content.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Spanish director Oliver Laxe questioned “if my cinema is Spanish or not,” and said that his Oscar-nominated film, “Sirat,” differed from the work of his Spanish peers.

item.title: Surge in Oil Prices Shakes Pakistan’s Already Fragile Economy
item.description: Families preparing for Eid al-Fitr and farmers ready for harvest are being squeezed in a country that gets nearly all its oil through the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arif Ali/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: A fuel storage site in Lahore, Pakistan, on Tuesday.

item.title: China’s Edge in an Oil Shock: Electric Cars and Renewables
item.description: Beijing’s decades-long push to reduce its dependence on foreign oil with huge investments in clean energy sources like electric vehicles is now paying off.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao

media:content.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Chinese demand for refined oil, gasoline and diesel fell last year as many people in China have switched to electric vehicles.

item.title: Corrections: March 14, 2026
item.description: Corrections that appeared in print on Saturday, March 14, 2026.

item.title: Quote of the Day: To Grasp Modern Dance, Think Like a Child
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Saturday, March 14, 2026.

item.title: Trump Issues Executive Orders to Tackle Housing Supply, Demand
item.description: The orders could undermine bipartisan legislation the Senate passed earlier this week, the most significant housing package in decades.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen

media:content.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Home prices are about 50 percent higher than they were at the start of the pandemic, and rents were up about 17 percent in December 2025 from December 2019, making housing affordability a primary concern of many voters.

item.title: Michigan Synagogue Attacker Killed Himself During Gunfight, Officials Say
item.description: The man who rammed his truck into a Michigan synagogue on Thursday killed himself during a firefight with security guards, law enforcement officials said.
item.dc:creator: Jackeline Luna

media:content.media:credit: Emily Elconin/Getty Images

item.title: Bosom Buddy
item.description: Kameron Austin Collins’s Saturday puzzle has tricks up its sleeve.
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Lovinger

media:content.media:credit: Roslan Rahman/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: “Thanks! It has pockets!”

item.title: TikTok Investors Set to Pay $10 Billion Fee to Trump Administration
item.description: The large fee is the latest example of the White House’s inserting itself into corporate deal making in unusual and aggressive ways.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Andrew Duehren

media:content.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The new investors paid the Treasury roughly $2.5 billion of the fee when the deal closed in January.

item.title: With Endorsement, Trump Clears Oklahoma Senate Path
item.description: The move, as other Republicans confirmed they would not run, all but assured Representative Kevin Hern would succeed Senator Markwayne Mullin.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein

media:content.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump endorsed Representative Kevin Hern in a social media post on Friday night and called him “a true friend of MAGA.”

item.title: Where Iran Is Hitting Back
item.description: Iran is retaliating against the U.S. with strikes on American sites throughout the Middle East. Our reporter Eric Schmitt looks at the damage these attacks have caused and what they tell us about Iran’s strategy.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Alexandra Ostasiewicz, Stephanie Swart, Leanne Abraham, Bora Erden and Pierre Kattar

item.title: Pentagon’s Boat Bombings Are Illegal, Human Rights Panel Is Told
item.description: Experts condemned the U.S. campaign at a human rights meeting in Guatemala, and the Trump administration condemned the meeting.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

media:content.media:credit: Moises Castillo/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Jamil Dakwar of the A.C.L.U. said the Pentagon’s attacks were being undertaken without the authorization of Congress and were “in violation of international law on the use of force.”

item.title: Jared Kushner Solicits Funds for His Firm While Working as Mideast Envoy
item.description: President Trump’s son-in-law is trying to raise $5 billion or more from foreign governments and others for his private equity firm.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Maureen Farrell
item.category: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington

media:content.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Jared Kushner, the founder of Affinity Partners, at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, in January.

item.title: New York Academy of Art Gives Away Money Donated by Jeffrey Epstein
item.description: The school also said that it would review policies about philanthropy and donor engagement after new revelations about the disgraced financier were made public.
item.dc:creator: Dionne Searcey

item.title: Trump’s War Alliance With Israel Is Reshaping the Middle East. But It Carries Risks.
item.description: President Trump is the first American leader to embrace fighting a full-fledged, joint war with Israel. Washington has tried to avoid that level of coordination in the past.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Donald J. Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel at Mar-a-Lago in December.

item.title: Here’s What Happened in the War in the Middle East on Friday
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Friday would be the most intense day of the U.S. air campaign in Iran as a vital oil supply route remained choked off.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A resident walks through a neighborhood of central Beirut, the capital of Lebanon, on Friday.

item.title: The Michigan Synagogue Attacker Was a Quiet Restaurant Worker
item.description: Days before the antisemitic violence, an imam recalled seeing Ayman Mohamad Ghazali at a service for his relatives who had been killed in the war in Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Kurt Streeter, Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs, Hwaida Saad and Neal E. Boudette
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Ayman Mohamad Ghazali, who officials said was killed after driving a car into Temple Israel in West Bloomfield Township, Mich., had stopped showing up to work in the days before the attack.

item.title: U.S. Attacks Iran’s Kharg Island, a Key Port for Oil Exporting, Trump Says
item.description: The strikes on Kharg Island targeted Iranian military forces, not economic infrastructure on the island, a U.S. military official said.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Farnaz Fassihi and Liam Stack
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Planet Labs PBC, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: A satellite image of Kharg Island in Iran last month.

item.title: Temple Israel Staff Raced to Save Lives in Michigan Synagogue Attack
item.description: “I didn’t breathe until I knew all of the kids were OK,” one teacher recalled after a truck crashed through the doors of a Michigan synagogue this week.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman and Katherine Rosman

media:content.media:credit: ABC Affiliate WXYZ, via Reuters
media:content.media:description: Smoke rising from Temple Israel in West Bloomfield, Mich., on Thursday.

item.title: Judge Blocks Trump Order Ending Deportation Protections for Somalis, for Now
item.description: The judge said she was staying the order indefinitely in large part because the Trump administration had been unprepared to defend it in court.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron

media:content.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Karmel Mall, a Somali-owned shopping center in Minneapolis, in January. The city was the focus of an aggressive immigration crackdown in Minnesota, the state with the largest population of Somalis.

item.title: Gunman in Old Dominion Shooting Appeared to Target ROTC, Court Records Show
item.description: Before opening fire in a university classroom, the gunman asked people if they were there for a Reserve Officers Training Corps event, court documents said.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: John Clark/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Law enforcement officers investigating the shooting at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Va., on Thursday.

item.title: Before Deadly Old Dominion Shooting, Parallel Lives of 2 Soldiers Diverged
item.description: Lt. Col. Brandon Shah flew hundreds of combat missions as a war-zone helicopter pilot before being shot and killed in his Old Dominion University classroom by a radicalized military veteran.
item.dc:creator: Dave Philipps
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Allen G. Breed/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: The authorities on Thursday investigated the scene at Constant Hall at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Va., where the shooting occurred.

item.title: After DOGE Deposition Videos Go Viral, Judge Orders Them Taken Down
item.description: The videos had been posted on YouTube by groups who were suing to restore grants cut by Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Jennifer Schuessler

media:content.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The building in Washington housing the National Endowment for the Humanities, where Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency helped carry out sweeping cuts last year.

item.title: Late-Winter Storm Set to Slam Upper Midwest This Weekend
item.description: The storm could be one of the strongest on record for parts of Minnesota and Wisconsin.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Messi Meets Trump and Argentina Is Divided
item.description: Lionel Messi, the Argentine soccer star, accompanied his team, Inter Miami, to the White House. The event turned into political dynamite back in Argentina.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Daniel Politi

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump with Lionel Messi and other members of Inter Miami in Washington last week.

item.title: The Fall of Noma’s René Redzepi Reverberates in the Restaurant World
item.description: In the industry where René Redzepi reigned, fellow chefs are debating how, and how much, restaurant kitchens can change.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin

media:content.media:credit: Signe Birck for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: René Redzepi stepped down Wednesday as the chef of Noma, the restaurant that made Copenhagen a global dining destination, after he was accused of past physical abuse.

item.title: Judge Temporarily Blocks Trump Demand for Student Race Data
item.description: A group of Democratic attorneys general had sued to overturn the Trump administration’s new policy that demanded the past seven years of student application data.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Vimal Patel

media:content.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Linda McMahon, the education secretary, at a briefing at the White House last year.

item.title: A New Magazine? In This Media Environment?
item.description: Now Voyager, an ambitious publication packed with dispatches from around the world, throws itself a party in Harlem.
item.dc:creator: Alex Vadukul

media:content.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The $29 debut issue, filled with international dispatches, and the totes to go with it.

item.title: Un nuevo enviado de Trump suscita temores de injerencia de EE. UU en las elecciones de Brasil
item.description: Darren Beattie, despedido del primer gobierno de Trump por asistir a una reunión de supremacistas blancos, se convirtió en el nuevo asesor principal del Departamento de Estado para la política hacia Brasil.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova

media:content.media:credit: Rebecca Noble para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Darren Beattie, quien recientemente se convirtió en el nuevo asesor principal del Departamento de Estado para la política hacia Brasil, fue despedido de un puesto de trabajo en el primer gobierno de Trump por aparecer en una conferencia a la que asistieron nacionalistas blancos.

item.title: Chemical Smell at Control Center Halts Traffic at Washington and Baltimore Airports
item.description: The ground delays, which also affected the airport serving Richmond, Va., were expected to last until at least midnight, according to the F.A.A., which said the smell had affected air traffic controllers.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk

media:content.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport was among the airports where the F.A.A. halted flights on Friday afternoon.

item.title: Charges Dropped Against Teenagers Whose Teacher Died in Prank Gone Wrong
item.description: The teacher’s relatives said they supported “getting the charges dropped for all involved” after a student prank led to his death last week in Georgia.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler

item.title: Justice Dept. Drops Prosecution of Veteran Who Burned American Flag
item.description: The veteran, Jay Carey, was arrested the same day that President Trump signed an executive order to punish flag burning, a First Amendment right.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi

media:content.media:credit: Rod Lamkey, Jr./Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Jay Carey with supporters after his arraignment in Washington in September. Mr. Carey had not been charged with flag burning itself but with two misdemeanors related to setting a fire on federal land.

item.title: Marines Issue Findings on California Shrapnel Incident
item.description: The October episode sent shrapnel onto California Highway Patrol vehicles on Interstate 5 during a celebratory exercise conducted for Vice President JD Vance and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay and Laurel Rosenhall
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Vice President JD Vance, left, watches the Marines conduct an amphibious exercise at Camp Pendleton’s Red Beach in October. During the event, shrapnel went flying onto a California Highway Patrol vehicle.

item.title: What to Know About AI Political Campaign Ads During Election Season
item.description: Wait, that’s not the real James Talarico …
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck

media:content.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The real James Talarico. Republicans recently released a video with an A.I.-generated version of him reading his old tweets.

item.title: Protesters Accused of Antifa Ties Found Guilty of Support for Terrorism
item.description: The guilty verdicts marked the first time that terrorism charges had been successfully brought against purported members of antifa.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Krista Torralva

media:content.media:credit: Louis DeLuca/The Dallas Morning News, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: The Prairieland Detention Center in Alvarado, Texas.

item.title: Iconic Pink Floyd Guitar Sells for a Record $14.55 Million
item.description: Pink Floyd Guitarist David Gilmour’s black Fender Stratocaster, which he played on six of the band’s albums, including “The Dark Side of the Moon,” broke the record for the most expensive guitar sold at auction.
item.dc:creator: Meg Felling
item.category: The Dark Side of the Moon (Album)

media:content.media:credit: Johannes Eisele/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: A Fender Stratocaster known as “The Black Strat,” part of the David Gilmour Guitar Collection, was displayed at Christie’s in New York City in 2019.

item.title: The U.S. Is Sending More Troops to the Middle East
item.description: Plus, Cuba is in talks with the U.S. Here’s the latest at the end of Friday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen

media:content.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Navy ships in Nagasaki, Japan, in 2023.

item.title: U.S. Vows to Block Iran’s Attempt to Shut Down Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz were “something we are dealing with.” And about 2,500 Marines were headed to the Middle East to bolster the war effort.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Luke Broadwater and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Thousands marched amid airstrikes on Quds Day, a government-sponsored event, on Friday in Tehran.

item.title: Mamdani Describes ‘Isolation’ and ‘Loneliness’ After Anti-Muslim Attacks
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani responded to Islamophobic comments from Senator Tommy Tuberville and a Texas congressman, saying they were dehumanizing Muslims.
item.dc:creator: Maya King

media:content.media:credit: Andres Kudacki/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani addressed the recent torrent of Islamophobic attacks during a Ramadan iftar meal with Muslim city workers at the Museum of the City of New York.

item.title: Bobby Douglas, Pathbreaking Olympic Wrestler, Dies at 83
item.description: In 1964, he was one of the first three African Americans to compete in wrestling at the Games. He went on to have a distinguished coaching career.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Judge Orders V.A. to Restore Union Contract With 300,000 Workers
item.description: Doug Collins, the V.A. secretary, had moved to nullify the contract in August, after Trump sought to eliminate collective-bargaining rights for federal workers.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague

media:content.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Doug Collins, the secretary of veterans affairs, on Capitol Hill last year.
